vehicles.name: Dealer Markup / Market Adjustment
vehicles.description: Added on top of MSRP during high-demand periods. Often fully negotiable or avoidable by shopping around.

vehicles.name: Documentation Fee
vehicles.description: Paperwork preparation fee that varies wildly by dealer. Ask for an itemized breakdown and push back on excessive charges.

vehicles.name: Nitrogen-Filled Tires
vehicles.description: Often sold as a premium safety or performance upgrade, but usually carries a steep markup for very limited practical benefit.

vehicles.name: Dealer-Installed Accessories
vehicles.description: Wheel locks, cargo mats, tint, alarms, and similar accessories are often preloaded onto the car and marked up far above market value. Ask for the addendum sheet before negotiating.

vehicles.name: Extended Warranty Bundles
vehicles.description: Packages that roll warranties, maintenance, and protections into the loan. Compare standalone pricing and skip unneeded coverage.

vehicles.name: Financing APR Markup
vehicles.description: Dealers can mark up your loan rate above the lender buy rate. Even a 1-2 point increase can add thousands over the life of the loan.

vehicles.name: Yo-Yo Financing / Spot Delivery
vehicles.description: You take the car home before financing is truly final, then get called back to sign at a worse rate or larger down payment. Do not assume the deal is final until funding is complete.

vehicles.name: Mandatory Protection Package
vehicles.description: Some stores present paint, fabric, theft, or maintenance packages as required. They usually are not required to buy the car.

vehicles.name: Fabric Protection
vehicles.description: Fabric, leather, stain, or interior protection products are commonly sold in the finance office at very high margins. They are rarely necessary at dealer pricing.

vehicles.name: Destination / Delivery Charges
vehicles.description: Factory destination charges on new cars are usually legitimate, but watch for duplicate freight, handling, or delivery fees added again by the dealer.

vehicles.name: Payment Packing
vehicles.description: The salesperson focuses on the monthly payment and quietly adds products or stretches the loan term. Always review the full out-the-door breakdown, APR, and term length.

vehicles.name: Gap Insurance
vehicles.description: Useful in some cases, but often cheaper through your insurer than the dealer.

vehicles.name: Trade-In Undervaluation
vehicles.description: A strong sale price can be offset by a weak trade-in offer. Always separate the new-car deal from the trade value when negotiating.

vehicles.name: Certified Pre-Owned Fee Premium
vehicles.description: CPO vehicles can be worth the premium, but some dealers charge far more than the warranty value justifies. Compare similar non-CPO listings.

vehicles.name: Open Recall or Incomplete Reconditioning
vehicles.description: A used vehicle may still need recall work, tires, brakes, or overdue service. Ask for the reconditioning report and recall status before signing.

vehicles.name: Salvage / Rebuilt Title History
vehicles.description: A rebuilt or salvage-history car can look fine but carry hidden structural, insurance, and resale risk. Verify title status and accident history independently.

vehicles.name: Tire or Brake Wear After Purchase
vehicles.description: A used car may look clean but still need tires, brakes, or a battery soon after purchase. Ask for tread depth, brake measurements, and service records.

vehicles.name: Second Key / Missing Accessories
vehicles.description: Replacing a missing smart key, charging cable, cargo cover, or floor mats can cost hundreds after delivery. Confirm included accessories in writing.

tips.vehicles: Get out-the-door quotes from multiple dealers, compare every line item, and separate the vehicle price, financing, trade-in, and add-ons before signing.

negotiation_tips: Shop at month-end or year-end when dealers push to hit quotas.
Get pre-approved financing from your bank or credit union before visiting the dealer.
Negotiate the out-the-door price, not the monthly payment.
Never discuss your trade-in until after you've agreed on the new car price.
Get written quotes from at least 3 dealers and use them against each other.
Say no to add-ons in the finance office — you can buy them cheaper elsewhere.
If the deal isn't right, walk away. There's always another car.
Review every line item on the buyer's order before signing.
